content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies like Galaxy quest, Princess bride -cleverly written light, funny adventurous movies

**Score:** 57
**Body:** Suggest me cleverly written movies like Galaxy quest and Princess bride, one is a scifi comedy and another is a fairlytale comedy. But both have clever writing. Light stress buster mood, no extreme violence no politics no extreme tragedy , just funny, cleverly written plot points, great character arcs, stakes, good setups and great payoffs, great world building and emotional

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stardust
2. Clue
3. Dungeons and Dragons: Honor Among Thieves

You don’t need to be into D&D to watch it, it’s a good movie
4. Big Trouble in Little China
5. Robin Hood: Men in Tights
6. The first Pirates of the Caribbean is a classic :)
7. Mystery Men
8. Spaceballs
9. Romancing the stone or stardust
10. Monty Python & the Holy Grail

Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves

Your Highness (\*may require herbal assistance for full humorous effect)

The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy

## 2. Best comedy movies

**Score:** 46
**Body:** Any movies that genuinely have made you laugh like really laugh? It’s been a while since I’ve found a good movie like that and was looking for some. Thanks in advance everyone 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. \- grandmas boy

\- old school

\- super troopers

\- how high

\- dumb & dumber
2. Overboard (1987)

Mouse Hunt

The Bucket List

Baby's Day Out

50 First Dates

Johnny Dangerously

King Ralph (1991)

Galaxy Quest

Top Secret

George of the Jungle

Accepted

Without a Paddle

Idiocracy

Spy 2015

Blue Streak

Liar Liar

Waterboy

The Beautician and the Beast

My friend likes: My Cousin Vinny  

I haven't seen Oscar (1991) yet
  - What a great list!
  - I watched Johnny Dangerously once.
3. Monty Python and the Holy Grail (1975)

Life of Brian (1979)

Airplane! (1980)

The Naked Gun Trilogy and 2025 film
4. Step Brothers
5. Best in Show (any of the Christopher Guest mockumentaries)
6. *All of Me* (1984) with Steve Martin & Lily Tomlin will, at times, make you snort. It's so good. Looks like it's free on Tubi right now, too.
7. Some I havent seen mentioned yet:

Happy Gilmore (dumb fun)

Hot Fuzz (funny buddy cop satire)

Any of the Jackass movies (gross out humor)

Mean Girls (teen comedy)

Napoleon Dynamite (one you can revisit yearly and always laugh)

Not Another Teen Movie (perfect satire)

Office Space (dunking on work culture)

Slither (horror comedy, if you like tucker and dale vs evil youll like this)

Walk Hard (funniest rip on music docs ever)

Wet Hot American Summer (silly satire)

What We Do in the Shadows (comedy about modern day vampires, the show is incredible as well)
8. A Fish Called Wanda

Coming to America

Trading Places

Animal House

Back to School

The Odd Couple II

Smokey and the Bandit

Avanti!

Some Like It Hot

Ferris Buellers Day Off

## 3. Must-see films from our childhood for today's kids

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I want to introduce my boys (7 and 5) to films from my childhood. Of course I've drawn a blank on what I used to watch. Here is my starting list:

\- ET  
\- Never ending story  
\- Star Wars films  
\- Labyrinth

No films which sexualize women or exaggerate in the portrayal of some nationalities.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Matilda
2. My kids were roughly that age, maybe a year older, when I showed them Honey I Shrunk the Kids. They loved it.
3. Land before time.
4. Goonies
5. Legend (1985)

The Dark Crystal

The Adventures of Baron Munchausen

Return to Oz
6. Secondhand Lions
7. Three great movies of my childhood (I must be older than you)

Mary Poppins (1964)

The Sound of Music (1965)

Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory (1971)
8. Adventures in Babysitting.
9. Harry and the Hendersons and Honey! I Shrunk the Kids are both favorites of my 6 year-old nephew.
10. Big, Home Alone, The Addams Family, Addams Family Values, Jumanji, Back to the Future, Goonies, Ghostbusters

## 4. Movies with a "gotcha" moment like Michael Clayton?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** This movie is so satisfying. I'm looking for other movies with a great story - thriller or otherwise - where one character has been working to "get" or expose another and at the end confronts them and reveals it's over for them. Thank you in advance! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **Black Bag**, 2025
  - Probably the most underrated movie I've seen this year. It's an excellent spy thriller
2. The Sting (1973).
3. **The Verdict**, 1982, with Paul Newman
4. Glengarry Glen Ross!
5. [removed]
6. Scott Tenorman Must Die
7. Duplicity (2009) - corporate espionage romcom that has a great "gotcha" climax.
8. *Malice* (1993), with Bill Pullman, Nicole Kidman, and Alec Baldwin, does this in a very satisfying fashion, albeit not quite as satisfyingly as *Michael Clayton*. That one is really top-notch.
9. The Girl With the Dragon Tattoo…tho the book did a better job with the gotcha part…the sequels also lead up to a large gotcha as well…they never finished the American version of the trilogy but the Swedish one is complete.

## 5. “Need movies with incredible spaceship interiors — that moody, claustrophobic vibe like Alien or Event Horizon”

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I’m obsessed with movies that make the inside of a spaceship feel real — like a place that’s been lived in, worked in, and might kill you if something goes wrong.

My top two examples are Alien (the GOAT of sci-fi atmosphere) and Event Horizon (terrifying, but that ship design is next level). I love those industrial corridors, flickering lights, the sound of machinery — that heavy, lonely mood.

Not just looking for horror — I’ll take any movie where the set design and lighting make you feel like you’re trapped in deep space.

What else scratches that same itch?

Bonus points if the ship itself feels like a character.


### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sunshine comes to mind. Stowaway, to a slightly lesser extent.

If you're open to series, The Expanse does this incredibly well.
  - If they're open to series then I would also suggest Battlestar Galactica
2. Moon (2009)
3. 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968)
4. The Martian
5. The Abyss
6. Pandorum
7. I also love the dirty version of space flight as opposed to the sterile version depicted in certain shows (eg, Star Trek). Serenity / Firefly are great for that.
8. - Aniara 
- High Life
9. Outland is a dark moody set . The Black Hole

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Meathead actor who turned in a surprisingly good performance?

**Score:** 661
**Body:** Stallone in Cop Land really exceeded my expectations, by playing against type.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Copland is so good. He’s great in it. One of my favorite deniro quotes is also in it, endlessly repeated in my friend group “you had your chance and you BLEWWW IT!”
  - Its so wild that its considered a failure too. I love it.
2. You know he's actually intelligent and well spoken right?  You're thinking of Rocky.
  - He wrote Rocky, insane quality script. 

I mean can you imagine Arnold, Van Damme, Steven Seagal writing a script like this early in their careers? It's crazy
  - Sly is a joy to watch on screen. He’s really good
3. [First Blood](https://youtu.be/oXngES_l_HQ?si=UCEAr-YO_rkB8PDe) (1982)

The best single example is Stallone’s [monologue](https://youtu.be/PtWHgkNH5yU?si=ba0ZajaVJh9oV_IG) at the end of the movie.
  - I still love that First Blood's first cut was so bad, Stallone tried to buy it back so it would never see the light of day. Then they did a huge reworking of the edit to really remove so much of the dialogue, which was really dumb. Apparently the woods part was 90 minutes long and filled with him saying horrific dialogue. Including mouthing off to a owl. The original cut was over 3 hours, and they cut half the movie, basically removing most of Stallone's dialogue and we got the great film that it was.
4. Stallone's a weird case. He looks like a meathead. He sounds like a meathead. But somehow, he is not a meathead.  
It's actually a shame he didn't do more serious acting work. When he was good, like in First Blood, and Rocky, and Copland, he was great. And the rest of the time? Rambo III, Cobra, Rocky IV, The Expendables, Judge Dread... fuck was he ever bad. You almost can't believe it's the same guy.
5. Copland was amazing and absolutely my favorite Stallone movie
6. You think the guy who wrote "Rocky," got it made, and then remained relevant, working, and successful for the subsequent 50 years is not smart?

## 2. Which movie production had an intense and difficult process and left the actors genuinely shaken? I'll go first:

**Score:** 598
**Body:** Shelley Duvall had to undergo numerous retakes and was subject to psychological and emotional torment from Stanley Kubrick on the set of The Shining (1980).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As for leaving the actors shaken the Wizard of Oz definitely.
  - Hollywood back then was basically survival horror for actors.
2. The Twilight Zone movie by John Landis . A Vic Morrow stunt accident ended with 2 children and Morrow dead  . I’m sure the whole cast and crew were shattered.
  - Spielberg ended his relationship with Landis over this.
    - Which makes sense if you remember that this stunt wasn't properly set up and that the kids weren't even meant to be in the film.


Very irresponsible
3. Apocalypse Now
  - Yes! So much so that there's a whole documentary about it.
4. The Abyss
  - My immediate answer. 

“At one point, Mary Elizabeth Mastrantonio had a physical and emotional breakdown on the set and on another occasion, Ed Harris burst into spontaneous sobbing while driving home. Cameron himself admitted, "I knew this was going to be a hard shoot, but even I had no idea just how hard. I don't ever want to go through this again".”

I think Mastrantonio had to take a long break after.

I do love this movie, and both actors as well are favorites
5. Rust

## 3. What's the worst movie you've ever watched recently?

**Score:** 560
**Body:** 2 hours of my life I'll never get back. What was the director thinking with the slow pacing of the movie with no plot whatsoever and suspense? Oh boy and the actors here Idris and Rebeccas talents were wasted on this Netflix low budget movie. Total snooze fest.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. War of The Worlds.. Made me appreciate the Tom Cruise version a lot more
  - The 1953 version is fantastic. The special effects obviously aren't modern (the Martian force fields look like bell jars for one thing), but it tells a terrifying story of human helplessness in the face of the Martian invaders.
  - Ice Cube: "I'll only do this movie if I can work from home"
2. Highest 2 Lowest. I went in excited. What a STINKER. 
  - Spike remade a classic (Kurosawa’s ‘High and Low’) into a jumbled mess of a movie. The cinematography, music, editing, direction, performances - I’ve never seen all of it suck at the same time as this one. And they wasted Denzel!!!
3. Latest Jurassic park movie. Can’t remember its full names because I am still trying to delete it from my mind.
4. The worst movie you’ve ever watched… recently?
  - What’s the first thing you remember last?
5. Fountain of Youth. It was glaringly lacking any kind of heart. Just by the numbers, phone it in tosh. Look, I can watch and enjoy an adventure romp. I liked Jungle Cruise. Not every film is going to be a deep and meaningful experience, sometimes it's just sloppy fun. But this one? It stunk of cynicism. I like the actors and yet I didn't buy a single second of the film.
6. Rebel Moon. I went into it without many expectations, except that it'd be at least watchable. And at the request of the fans of the movie, I watched to two-part director's cuts. 

It's just ridiculous. The entire premise. The conflict, the way the good guys behave, the way the bad guys behave. The dialogue was dry, and sounded like it was written by a 12 year old whose only direction was "make everyone brooding teenagers." The good guys have to hurry before the bad guys get there, so let's make ten pit stops to pick up random people. "Why are they picking up this guy? Because the director figured he'd look cool fighting enemies later in the seventeenth act." 

It's just bad. Even without the seemingly 40-minute wheat harvesting montage/documentary. It could've been a fairly decent sci-fi movie, but it ended up being six hours of "why have they done that? It makes literally zero sense." I get that "the rule of cool" sometimes takes over, but first off, it needs to actually be cool. And second off, it doesn't need to dictate every aspect of the story. Some things are best done with a semblance of what makes sense.

## 4. If Philip Seymour Hoffman is in a movie, it's a guarantee I'm watching it.

**Score:** 517

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His death was such a waste.
  - keeps surprising me when I see him on screen and have to remind myself
2. What I love about actors like this, is they have to generate "Star power" from nothing.   
Someone like Jared Leto looks like a movie star. He can walk into a scene and draw eyes with his "aura". Without the look, Jared Leto would likely be a nobody or convict.   
Hoffman "aura" is negative from a photo alone. But the man has so much talent he takes over any movie. He literally steals your attention through pure acting alone. Amazing talent!
3. **Met him in real life** when I was an extra in The Master.  He wasn't shooting that day and was still helping out move set pieces, cameras, etc.. I told him that it was awesome that he did that without having to be on set that day. He told me, *"We are all a part of the filmmaking process, and he tries to help in whichever way he can, while learning something new and making a great movie."  Rest in Power, Phillip*
4. Twister
  - The suck zone!
5. Brandt can't watch, though. Or he has to pay a hundred.
6. Tommy, how’s the peeping…
7. Fuckin loved him in Charlie Wilson’s War. His opening scene is just so good
8. Til The Devil Knows You're Dead

## 5. Napoleon Dynamite. Yay or Nay?

**Score:** 506

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ve always considered it a masterpiece.
  - As someone who saw it in theaters, it was so refreshingly weird and quirky. It sort of came out of nowhere.
    - I was living in Hollywood and my parents from Idaho were coming for a visit and I was scrambling to find something to do with them, and I saw a PG (!) movie about Idaho was playing one 1 screen at the Sunset 5, so I took them there opening weekend and everyone in the theater was falling out of their seats laughing, while my parents from Idaho sat there stone-faced the entire time like this was a serious dramatic piece.
  - if you didn't, you'd be gettin' a roundhouse 🇺🇸👖🇺🇸
2. Love it, but it's not for everyone. It came out at the right time. I'm not sure what it would be like to see it for the first time now
  - I watched it for the first time this month. It took maybe 15 minutes to settle into the style of humour but after that i had a blast. I think the humour is more timeless than some people think.
3. Yay!
4. It should be in the Library of Congress as a cultural relevant piece of visual media. It is by far the best of its class of satires/comedies. The fact that its memes still hit today is evidence of its impact. It feels like a cult classic movie except it was wildly popular from the get go. 
5. Yay, i love this movie
6. Big yay !

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Inland Empire: why Poland?

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I cant post in r/davidlynch due to lack of karma in that sub, so im posing the question here.

In my opinion, Inland Empire is Lynch reckoning with his place in Hollywood as a male director and the power that he holds over actresses and the inherent exploitation of actresses in the industry. It's a sort of apology to people like Sherilyn Fenn, Sheryl Lee, Laura Flynn Boyle, and Laura Harring, actresses whose careers were cut short in part due to the inherent objectification and boxing-in of them for being in popular Lynch projects. It compares the way Hollywood uses actresses to prostitution and explores how acting inherently creates an "evil" reflection of them in both the film and its surrounding media and press that haunts them forever.

But what I can't understand is, why Poland in particular? Why are the prositutes (atleast in some scenes) and Nikki's husband Polish? Does it have some history I don't know that relates to this? Does it have to do with the film they're working on being "based on a gypsy folktale"? Does Lynch just have beef with Poland? I'd love to hear your thoughts.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kristine McKenna talks about it in *Room to Dream*. Lynch fell in love with Poland, and specifically the city of Łódź, when he became involved in the Camerimage Festival during the post-production of *Mulholland Drive*. He made friends there who helped him find actors and scout locations. Here’s a quote in the book from Marek Zebrowski:

>David fell in love with Łódź and started getting all kinds of ideas. The winter atmosphere, abandoned factories, opulent residences from the late nineteenth century—all these things came together to create the beautifully mysterious film *INLAND EMPIRE*… (p. 376)
  - Fun fact they were slated to give him an apartment in a renovated factory, toured that sucker in 2013-2014. Allegedly he said no other place captured the vibe of old New York.
2. Being occasionally child-like in his worldview, I wouldn't be surprised if Lynch simply found Poland to be strange and mystical in the way Americans often think of European folk culture.
3. I believe he was invited to a film festival in some city in Poland, and liked the look of the city so much that he decided to film there. I don't think there's much more to it than that. Focus on the donut, not the hole.
4. I'm a Lynch fan from Poland and the city the Camerimage Festival was held in (either Torun or Bydgoszcz) is very different to the city of Łódź. Łódź is famously know as one of the post-industrial cities that no one wants to live in for more than a few years. Lots of ruined buildings, factories and chimneys, gray walls and such. We call it the bum city, even though it has the greatest film school (Wajda, Kieslowski). It's close enough to the capital city Warszawa / Warsaw, so Łódź has also been dubbed the bedroom of Warsaw. 


You know, it just gives this feeling of a city that was great in it's time, but it has since passed, it stopped progressing. Up to late 2010s it was frozen in time. Knowing everything industrial is close to Lynch's 'aesthetic' and the plot of the film concerns the past, I guess he just felt connected to it. I'm not sure if USA has places that could portray early 20th century as well.


There was also a major gallery show of Lynch's artworks in Toruń and Twin Peaks was very popular here in the 90s (although mostly as a soap opera). I also heard he was supposed to buy a studio in Łódź in and old building, but there were some shenannigans with the city major and city architects or something.
5. I think you should rewatch the movie... 

It's largely about a woman committing an act she views as wrong and holding herself karmically responsible. (and eventually releasing herself from that karmic debt, so I don't get how you could get "haunted forever" from it.)

And maybe rewatch all Lynch movies to get a better view of how he treats female characters and actresses.
  - okay, whether she releases herself or not (i would disagree, she has to die to face the phantom and sets free a younger girl who is watching her life/work on tv, its about change over time and the way previously exploited actresses have opened up avenues for younger women to not have to deal with as bad of the same problems as they did, but only AFTER they've had their careers derailed), it is absolutely about the exploitation of actresses and i dont understand how you could see it any other way. nikki has no autonomy, her husband is incredibly overbearing and controlling of her social life and who she interacts with (threatening to kill devon etc.) and lynch explicitly parallels her experiences in hollywood with old world prostitutes and domestic violence victims, both groups of women who are exploited at the hands of men and are rejected autonomy.

not to mention that the phantom appears as a distorted version of her face after she sees her film on screen, and her sense of self is changed through having to embody this character in a role that is receiving media attention, and she is inherently sexualized by the interviewer at the beginning.
6. Just like some said Lynch really liked Camera image festival in Łódź. And not surprisingly because Lynch was very popular director in Poland especially in 90s and 2000s. He was cult icon here at least among film fans firstly thanks to twin peaks.
7. >But what I can't understand is, why Poland in particular? Why are the prositutes (atleast in some scenes) and Nikki's husband Polish? **Does it have some history** I don't know that relates to this?

First and foremost, Poland would have been a country it was possible to produce a film more cheaply back when INLAND EMPIRE was made around 2005. I have no doubt this factor played a big part.

Aside from that, as others have pointed out it may have been an unconscious decision on Lynch's part brought about by the sympathy or attraction he felt when travelling in Poland.

My theory: Poland is a country whose history connects intimate family trauma with broadband atrocity.

Every Polish person I've met born last century had family trauma. Poland as a country is preoccupied with its trauma, traded off between German and Russian empires, and having been made the geography of the Holocaust—more or less willingly, depending who you ask.

The resentment of this history informs this century's successes of the far right party, PiS ("Prawo i Sprawiedliwość" or "Law and Order"). Poland is a country in which you can find huge pro-life photographic billboards depicting dead foetuses on the outskirts of the major cities.

Put simply, Poland is rich territory for Lynch's thematic preoccupations. In some sense Poland is a whole nation of Palmer families, even if many of these families have been less well off than Lynch's US white picket fence comfort zone.

I should add that I love Poland. But jakoś to będzie …
  - this makes complete sense and im kinda sad i didnt realize this sooner given my Kieslowski obsession. thank you for the reply!!

## 2. Film appreciation: the tragic performer + Letterboxd list

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Lately I've been obsessed with films that depict people in the search of fame, status, art and desire, but who encounter only tragedy. The breakage of the dream, the departure from reality, the collapse of the hedonistic pursue of a heightened life. It's like a punishment from god for aiming too hard towards something that is covertly corrupted.

Mulholland Drive (2001) and Sunset Boulevard (1950) are perfect examples. Actresses who desire to be big stars in Hollywood, but not only they're rejected, their psyche falls apart as they go. What Ever Happened to Baby Jane (1962) is another example of the golden child who grew up emotionally inept and traumatized, who desires to go back to the spotlight desperately, despite not being up to measure.

On the other hand, Velvet Goldmine (1998) and The Doors (1991) explore the idea of identity and recognition through flamboyant performances and narcissistic characters.

Do you like these types of films? Are there other films worth talking about? I'm curating a list on Letterboxd called "Fatal Histrion" in which I include the ones I mentioned and more.


### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Red Shoes and to a lesser extent All About Eve. Black Swan, which is heavily influenced by The Red Shoes is a more recent example. A very commercialized version could be Wall Street.

Just remembered this unforgettable film by Kazan, A Face in the Crowd.
  - Interesting that, out of the two Oliver Stone movies, you picked Wall Street and not Talk Radio, which fits the template better. Eric Bogosian's character is a performer of the airwaves, kills his personal life for the sake of a better slot for his show and ends up getting killed himself by a member of his audience.
    - Yea, good catch, and I simply forgot about that film. I would have to rewatch it since I haven't seen it since it was released. It was very relevant, as was Wall St., for the time, but I found it a bit too bombastic and sensationalist, even if was reflecting the outrageous nature of the content.

King of Comedy is a film that I prefer more if one were to compare the two.
2. *La Strada* is a bit of a poster child for this genre, I’d say. Olivier in *The Entertainer*, Renoir’s *The Golden Coach*. And of course *The Day the Clown Cried*, if you can find a copy.
3. The Black Dahlia 

Blonde 

The Canyons

A few fifties films, which you’d have research yourself whether they apply:

The Country Girl (I haven’t seen it yet, but will be watching soon, so I don’t want to look up the plot);

Sweet Bird of Youth (don’t know, haven’t seen it);

Carrie, with Jennifer Jones and Laurence Olivier (don’t know if sufficiently tragic in the punishing sense for your criteria).

## 3. Documentary Approaches to Everyday Labor: Observing the Invisible

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I’ve been thinking about how documentary films handle the depiction of everyday, often overlooked work. Many filmmakers struggle to balance authenticity and engagement when portraying labor that’s essential but rarely noticed. Some series I’ve come across, including projects like People Worth Caring About, attempt to bring these invisible roles into focus by showing the rhythms, challenges, and small victories of people in caregiving, trades, and waste management.

What I find compelling is the reliance on observational techniques and intimate storytelling rather than heavy narration or dramatization. It raises questions about cinematic ethics and style: how can filmmakers maintain audience interest without distorting reality? And how do you create empathy for subjects whose work is mundane in daily life but profound in social value?

I’m curious if others have noticed similar approaches in cinema or documentary series. Are there films that capture ordinary labor with the same subtlety and respect, and what narrative strategies make these portrayals emotionally resonant without feeling manipulative?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wang Bing uses endurance. Long takes, right up there with people in terrible working situations, letting them speak, panoramic establising shots contextualising the sense of lunar isolation of some of the crazy industrial places he films. I don't think he's worried about maintaining interest: the intensity of the images and their essential truth carries so much weight.

On the other hand Michael Glawogger used lots of very stylised close-ups, giving a sense of labour economies as a kind of biological network, anonymising individual stories and giving a sense of the repetition and sheer amount of labour being undertaken.

## 4. I thought GDT's Frankenstein was...bad

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Somehow devoid of a personality that isn't a laundry list of Gothic aesthetic cliches, and drenched in del Toro's typical Y2K Hollywood blockbuster aesthetic (are we still on the teal and orange hype? really?), this rendition of Frankenstein is a trite and ploddy film with a terrible script that sinks what I'm sure is a wonderful source novel.

Now I've not read the novel so forgive me if any of my criticisms I'm levelling at the hack that is del Toro are actually Mary's handywork, but the script in this is utter dogshit. It is devoid of any forward momentum and the character arc is a bullet-point rendition of an arc: jagged and janky. There's not enough threading between the points for them to make sense, so we have Victor as an impassioned scientist desperate to prove that death can be beaten because of the loss of his Mother as a child, and then once he actually manages to do this, he very suddenly starts hating his creation because...he can only say one word? That is your metric for success now, Victor? I thought you were trying to reckon with death itself, yet when you successfully animate a corpse made of the body parts of many, and when that creature exhibits clear intelligence and emotional capacity, you ignore all of that and get wound up because he keeps saying your name?

There wasn't enough seeding of Victor's character to justify his sadism, except that the film needed to create an adverserial relationship between the two to justify the cold open and give the film some element of conflict. But this conflict is completely unearned. Victor is needlessly cruel and I was baffled as to why he was reacting that way. Any thematic reason is lost in the sauce of golden-hour lighting and an obsession with the romantic, but without the emotional stakes or depth between the characters to prop this up as more than just a finger-puppet exercise.

Don't even get me started on Mia Goths character. Fucking hell. The one female character we get is the one who sees the beauty in the beast, and treats him with kindness that no one else does (because only women have empathy right right right???). She has no development (none of the characters do really) but it's egregious here because her entire personality is designed only as a foil to Victor - he's interested in the grand questions of life and death, she's interested in the infinitesimal of the world - so the only female character we get is a generic "I see you for who you really are" lady whose personality is centred around the male protagonist. I swear if people didn't have a random boner for del Toro, and if this exact script were written by any other male writer, we'd be shitting all over this. Her death is so magnificently cliche in the worst fucking way (fucking Friends was lampooning this trope in the goddamn 90s, and that is a shit show) that it serves only to illuminate how emotionally bankrupt this film actually is. Bookend this stupid death scene with Victor's brother literalising a theme so often explored in any media whatsoever involving any humanoid monster whatsoever - "You're the monster, Victor" - and you have the perfect recipe for an utter shitstorm of wanky filmmaking.

This is why I don't like del Toro. He masquerades auterism behind his good production designers, and though it's clear there's passion in what he does, he's not a kid, so I'm not going to applaud him for the bad drawing he's brought home from school, and it is certainly not going on my fucking fridge.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. De Toro's fetishism for innocent monsters was more of a detriment in this movie compared to any of his other films. The book is less black and white, Victor and the Monster are both terrible beings. GDT really sanitizes the Monster and makes him more of a tragic hero. In the book, the monster kills Elizabeth and he also slowly kills Victor's family to make him as lonely as himself. He's very petty, violent and angry. You lose a lot of that greyness in the film. I almost laughed out loud when Victor's brother looked at the camera and told Victor that he was the monster. Not very subtle at all. 

There is an underlying theme of generational trauma that GDT added. The stuff with Victor's father and him in return being abusive to the Monster. But that felt basic and not expanded upon.
2. Many of your criticisms are spot on but Victor becoming disinterested in his creation is... Just kind of the premise.

Victor's behavior towards the monster reflects his owns fathers behaviors towards him. The exact same reactions, the same whipping etc. In an utterly unsubtle film, this is pretty direct.

Everything with Frankenstein brother is a construction do the film and and an odd over complication.  The "you are the monster" moment is quite bad indeed.  

While lot of death and chaos result from Victor's efforts, AI think much of the true damage is undercut by del Toro never really allowing the Creature to become as twisted as he is in the book. The Creature is treated by a cruel world and is...mostly fine? Save for throwing Victor around he never commits any violence against anyone who isn't attacking him first.

The movie exhausts a lot of runtime in oddly being the Batman Begins of Frankenstein films. Losing his mom and his own father cruelty as an acolanaitionnfornVictor Frankenstein is rather pat but serviceabke. Exploriginnthr logistics of where Victor got his lab and materials is an odd choice. "At least we'll have spares" could have been appropriated in reference to battlefield corpses..

Mostly all that screentime spent there short changes the later half of the story, which gets rather rushed.
3. I actually thought it was a decent spin on the source material. However, I think it's clear it got 'Netflixed' e.g., deliberately dumbed down so people can watch while scrolling, which I think is why the dialogue was so on-the-nose sometimes (especially the "You are the monster" line). I can excuse that since the story isn’t meant to be especially ambiguous. I liked the setting, mood, and existential themes, but it wasn’t groundbreaking.
  - This movie would be exactly same if made for Fox or Universal. Del Toro is just not a very good writer. See Also:  Crimson Peak.
4. I think there is a good review in here, but my god the amount of antipathy you show towards del Toro is just frankly unearned.

Chronos, The Devil's Backbone, Pan's Labyrinth, Hellboy 1/2, Shape of Water, hell even Pacific Rim are all worthwhile films in their own right.

And even in his lesser films like Mimic, Crimson Peak, Nightmare Alley or Pinnochio there is plenty to enjoy and bask in.

He is definitely hot and cold and never been one for subtlety, but neither has Spielberg and he isn't a "hack" as you call del Toro here.

He is a maximalist director that wears his heart on his sleeve. When he is directly addressing topics that affect him dearly --- familial strife, his mexican/spanish heritage, the beauty in the bizarre --- he can make some genuinely moving pieces. 

I haven't seen Frankenstein, but I can't tell if this review is an accurate assessment because the hate towards del Toro is apparent in every line.
5. GDT has had increasingly diminishing returns with almost all his films in chronological order. At this point he’s basically a great set designer, not a director of merit at all. Frankenstein is also quite a nuanced story, far too subtle for him.
  - Lol, that’s what I thought when leaving the theater: “This guy’s really been milking *Pan’s Labyrinth* for almost two decades.” He’s not bad, but his reputation exceeds him at this point.

## 5. Was "One Battle After Another" trying to say anything?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Because I don't know if it was.

Obviously it was "about" a lot of things, in the sense that a lot of things happen or are depicted related to many current and age-old political issues.

But for the life of me, I never got the sense that ideas of revolution, racism, or parenthood were ever the driving forces of the plot. They were all there, but it felt like the plot was just happening around them.

Willa:

I found here to be a remarkably passive character in the movie. She's establsihed as a basic "I hate my loser dad, and I have to be the adult in the house" archetype (enhanced by the fact that she's the black daughter of a white dad), but we don't reallye xplore that, and we defintiely don't learn much about how she feels about her mother, or her revolutionary past. We're told later on, by other characters, that she thought her mom was a hero and didn't know she snitched, and then we don't see her come to terms with that fact.

The only time she's given an interesting choice related to the French 75 and all that is at her school when the other lady shows up to save her, and she has to make the choice to trust her crazy dad about all the secret codes and revolutionary underground. But from that moment on, all the choices she makes are just about her trying to survive direct physical danger.

And then we learn that she's actually the daughter of Lockjaw, and, ok? Again, her relationship to Bob is pretty underdevelopped, but we trust that she kinda loves him as her father, so what does this revelation that Sean Penn is really her father actually change for her? Not much.

Revolution?

I also couldn't help but feel the revolutionary themes were pretty surface level. What do the characters fight for? What ideology unites or divides them? We see plenty of latin american immigrants being being freed and smuggled away from the military (based and all) but the movie doesn't spend any time showing us the suffering these people are trying to escape, or awaits them if they ge turned back (except for a handfull of scenes that are set in/near detention facilities, but again, don't really focus on the real pain that goes on there)

the closest thing to a real "statement" was with the Lockjaw character, whose role can be boiled down to the idea that "white supremacist christian nationalists are just a bunch of pathetic creeps only fueled by hatred and lust, and treat even each other as disposable trash" (again, based). But it his character was basically just THAT throughout the whole movie, no slow revelation of his depravity, no exploration or maybe humanization. He's just a psycho creep from beginning to end.

Now, you might say "it's not trying to be some basic father daughter movie!" "It's not the movies job to convince you that mass deportations are bad!!"

And that's fine, PTA can make his movie however, and about whetever, but then what is the movie trying to do?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

the revolutionary aspect is clearly pretty background and from the first act it’s established that the thing Ghetto Pat is fighting for is his daughter. 

PTA films generally are not about ideals or morals or meaning, but about human beings, whether they’re broken and relatable (Magnolia) or hilarious cartoon characters (Inherent Vice). he’s not really a “sentimental message” kind of filmmaker. mostly his films are just entertaining but with a bit more depth and artistic sensibility than you’re average entertaining filmmaker.

The more I think about it, the more I become convinced this is mainly a "girl-dad" movie about parental anxieties and accepting your kids finding their place in the world, rather than some deep treatise on the state of American politics (and if it is meant to be the latter, I think it kinda misses the mark).

It's more Finding Nemo than it is Battle of Algiers. And I don't mean that as a bad thing.
5. One of the themes I came away thinking about was how you prove your identity. The two organizations', French 75 and Christmas Adventures, are both concerned about demonstrating membership, but one cares about shared experience (passwords, knowledge checks) and the other cares about blood. 


That's exactly what Willa is facing when she has to consider who her "real" father is, the one who raised her, or the one she shares DNA with. PTA returns to the theme of fatherhood and family and it makes sense that's what he would focus on from Vineland. 


I think it even picks a side, at the end when Willa sees Bob, she can clearly tell it's him but she asks for the password and he tries to refuse, it's like she's saying prove you're my father and he's responding I don't have to, that's something you get to decide.
6. To me, community is the film’s emotional engine. Here, Anderson celebrates the improvisational labor that keeps movements alive, while also cataloguing the internal rot beguiling revolutionary movements: purity tests, performative solidarity, and trauma calcifying into doctrine are satirized to devastating effect. These aren’t abstractions, however: these are very human failures and compromises, which is why the critique of this revolutionary energy lands with both force and compassion.

On that note: the Bob / Sensei sequence, with Bob fussing over a dead phone while the Sensei is literally corralling and saving his community, is a masterclass in how the film collapses the personal and the political into a devastating microcosm. It’s one of the funniest scenes of the year, but the real payoff is the emotional core of this scene; a community leader saving the people that he loves. He makes it look effortless, but the immense planning and years of care is implied. That scene says, in miniature, what the film argues on a grander scale: That revolutions are not primarily fought in the streets, they are truly sustained by the small, ordinary acts of unselfish, organized care for your community.

To me, the Sensei is therefore the moral fulcrum of the film. He’s a steady practitioner of communal repair, he embodies the film’s thesis that true leadership begins and ends with caretaking. His presence reframes the other characters’ choices; and he is the moral compass through which the other characters are measured. With the Sensei as our guiding moral core, we see very plainly the contradictions of nearly every other character in the film: who here is willing to trade power for preservation and who confuses domination with defense. Importantly, the Sensei isn’t a one-note paragon. He is decisive and a bit tired, straightforward and strong yet tender. This emotional core is aspirational, and we see glimmers of it in the other characters, only to be strengthened as the film progresses. There has been no greater cinematic joy for me this year than seeing the effects of the kindness and care of these characters rippling through this world.

Lockjaw, by contrast, operates as a dark mirror. Our moral duty of holding communities together is constantly threatened by his warpath. He is overtly villainous; yes, but even scarier is his self-justifying cynicism illustrating the mechanics of systemic harm that plague communities on a daily basis. His psyche is calibrated to explain away cruelty as realism, to reduce solidarity into cold, calculated leverage. Watching him is watching the ways in which the evils of the world legitimize themselves in plain sight. We see opportunism dressed as pragmatism and exploitation rationalized as societal necessity. This villain shows plainly how corruption spreads quietly, not always with fanfare but with a thousand polite accommodations from people too scared to say no. Lockjaw is so chilling because he is so recognizably human. He’s the logic many societal institutions adopt when conscience is expensive and inconvenient.

The film privileges messy, communal repair instead of tidy resolutions of conflict. In our era when spectacle often substitutes for convincing argument, One Battle After Another fully understands how power shifts, how communities hold each other up, and why revolutions with good intentions often fail. Apathy, complacency, and selfishness are cancers that can come for us all, even the most forward-thinking and dedicated of us. Bob’s quote about “not getting mad about anything anymore,” at almost exactly the midpoint of the film, perfectly illustrates the film’s forceful case towards taking a stand for what you believe in before it’s too late. 
7. I think directly addressing themes makes a work of art less interesting and engaging. It's not the artist's job to tell you what to think. Deriving meaning is at least partially the job of the audience. And that's part of the fun of a film like this- different themes are going to resonate differently from person to person. I find your use of the word "trying" interesting, because I think films (or any works of art) that are trying to say something specific are usually less compelling than the ones that leave some of that work to the audience.

One thing that I took away from it was that a possible reason for failure of the previous generation's revolution was because the revolutionaries' motivations and aspirations were tied their individual desires and expressions (aesthetic, sexual, political, etc.).  It was not a collective, it was a collection of individuals.  Bob was there because he liked black girls.  Perfidia Beverly Hills was there to empower Perfidia Beverly Hills, not others.


There's also something about how the revolution and the oppression are infatuated with each other, and spur each other on.

10. I've never got the impression that any of PTA's movies are "trying to say anything". They're films which take a premise, or a character, or a situation, and explore it - often in quite a vague or disjointed way.
